perturbation.original_text: 1.1 Net Investment Income. "Fund Investment Income", as defined in paragraph 1.2 below, minus "Fund Operating Expenses" as defined in paragraph 1.3 below equals Net Investment Income. If the Fund's Net Investment Income on any day is below zero, the Investment Adviser shall waive its Advisory Fee or reimburse the Fund an amount (defined as "Expense Waiver") sufficient to produce a Net Investment Income of zero.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 Net Investment Income. "Fund Investment Income", as defined in paragraph 1.2 below, minus "Fund Operating Expenses" as defined in paragraph 1.3 below equals Net Investment Income. If the Fund's Net Investment Income on any day is below zero, the Investment Adviser shall waive its Advisory Fee or reimburse the Fund an amount (defined as "Expense Waiver") sufficient to produce a Net Investment Income that they deem reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement from bringing the Net Investment Income to zero to a "reasonable" amount introduces ambiguity and contradicts the intended purpose of maintaining a minimum income level for the fund.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 36(b) imposes a fiduciary duty on investment advisers regarding compensation received from a registered investment company. By introducing the term 'reasonable' instead of the clearly defined 'zero,' the modification creates uncertainty about whether the advisory fee waivers and reimbursements are fulfilling the fiduciary duty to protect the fund and its shareholders, potentially violating this section.

Breach of fiduciary duty (a) Civil actions by Commission; jurisdiction; allegations; injunctive or other relief The Commission is authorized to bring an action in the proper district court of the United States, or in the United States court of any territory or other place subject to the jurisdiction of the United States, alleging that a person who is, or at the time of the alleged misconduct was,
perturbation.justification: The change from 'zero' to 'reasonable' introduces ambiguity and directly contradicts the objective standard initially set for Net Investment Income. The original text mandates a specific outcome (Net Investment Income of zero), while the changed text introduces a subjective standard ('reasonable') that could potentially allow the Investment Adviser to not fully reimburse the Fund. The Law Explanation states this could violate the fiduciary duty to protect the fund and its shareholders under Section 36(b). Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 80a-35, addresses 'Breach of fiduciary duty,' indicating the legal relevance of maintaining clear and enforceable standards in investment agreements. The altered text's ambiguity could be interpreted as a weakening of the adviser's obligation, potentially failing to meet the fiduciary standard.

perturbation.original_text: Fund Operating Expenses are the aggregate expenses of every character incurred by the Fund, including but not limited to investment advisory fees of the Investment Adviser (but excluding interest, taxes, brokerage commissions and other expenditures which are capitalized in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles and other extraordinary expenses not incurred in the ordinary course of the Fund's business).
perturbation.changed_text: Fund Operating Expenses are the aggregate expenses of every character incurred by the Fund, including all investment advisory fees of the Investment Adviser, including interest, taxes, brokerage commissions.
perturbation.explanation: This change broadens the definition of 'Fund Operating Expenses' to explicitly include items (interest, taxes, brokerage commissions) that are typically excluded under generally accepted accounting principles, which contradicts standard accounting practices used in fund management and reporting.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 210.6-04 outlines the requirements for the form and content of financial statements for investment companies. Specifically, it details the components of the statement of operations, which implicitly relies on GAAP to define what constitutes operating expenses. The modified text, by including items like interest and taxes within operating expenses when GAAP typically excludes them, could violate the reporting requirements under this SEC regulation and potentially mislead investors about the true operating costs of the fund.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 210.6-04 - Balance sheets. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 210.6-04 - Balance sheets. CFR prev | next § 210.6-04 Balance sheets. This section is applicable to balance sheets filed by registered investment companies and business development companies except for persons who substitute a statement of net assets in accordance with the requirements specified in § 210.6-05 , and issuers of face-amount certificates which are subject to the special provisions of § 210.6-06 . Balance sheets filed under this rule shall comply with the following provisions: Assets 1. Investments in securities of unaffiliated issuers. 2. Investments in and advances to affiliates. State separately investments in and advances to: (a) Controlled companies and (b) other affiliates. 3. Other investments. State separately amounts of assets related to (a) variation margin receivable on futures contracts, (b) forward foreign currency contracts; (c) swap contracts; and (d) investments—other than those presented in §§ 210.12-12 , 12-12A, 12-12B, 12-13, 12-13A, 12-13B, and 12-13C. 4. Cash. Include under this caption cash on hand and demand deposits. Provide in a note to the financial statements the information required under § 210.5-02 .1 regarding restrictions and compensating balances. 5. Receivables. (a) State separately amounts receivable from (1) sales of investments; (2) subscriptions to capital shares; (3) dividends and interest; (4) directors and officers; and (5) others. (b) If the aggregate amount of notes receivable exceeds 10 percent of the aggregate amount of receivables, the above information shall be set forth separately, in the balance sheet or in a note thereto, for accounts receivable and notes receivable. 6. Deposits for securities sold short and other investments. State separately amounts held by others in connection with: (a) Short sales; (b) open option contracts (c) futu
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language directly contradicts standard accounting practices, potentially violating SEC regulations. The original text explicitly excludes 'interest, taxes, brokerage commissions,' aligning with GAAP. The changed text includes them, misaligning with GAAP, which 17 CFR § 210.6-04 implicitly relies on for defining operating expenses. This is not a minor nuance but a direct contradiction of standard practices, potentially misleading investors as per the Law Explanation. Snippet 2 refers to balance sheets, which are impacted by how operating expenses are defined.

perturbation.original_text: Any Expense Waiver recovery payment by the Fund to the Investment Adviser shall not cause the Operating Expense for the Fund's full year of operations to exceed 1.25% of the Fund's average daily net assets.
perturbation.changed_text: Any Expense Waiver recovery payment by the Fund to the Investment Adviser shall not cause the total expenses for the Fund's full year of operations to exceed a percentage deemed appropriate by the Trust's board.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a fixed percentage limit (1.25%) with a subjective standard ('a percentage deemed appropriate by the Trust's board') introduces ambiguity and removes a clear constraint on expense recovery, potentially impacting the fund's compliance with expense limitations required by regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 12(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 relates to distribution plans and necessitates certain controls to prevent excessive or inappropriate charges. This modification replaces a hard cap with a subjective determination by the board. Removing the objective standard and replacing it with board discretion could allow for excessive expense charges, thereby potentially violating Section 12(b) and associated SEC rules regarding fair and reasonable expense management within the fund.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-12 - Functions and activities of investment companies | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-12 - Functions and activities of investment companies U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Purchase of securities on margin; joint trading accounts; short sales of securities; exceptions It shall be unlawful for any registered investment company , in contravention of such rules and regulations or orders as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors— (1) to purchase any security on margin, except such short-term credits as are necessary for the clearance of transactions; (2) to participate on a joint or a joint and several basis in any trading account in securities, except in connection with an underwriting in which such registered company is a participant; or (3) to effect a short sale of any security, except in connection with an underwriting in which such registered company is a participant. (b) Distribution by investment company of securities of which it is issuer It shall be unlawful for any registered open-end company (other than a company complying with the provisions of section 80a–10(d) of this title ) to act as a distributor of securities of which it is the issuer, except through an underwriter, in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (c) Limitations on commitments as underwriter It shall be unlawful for any registered diversified company to make any commitment as underwriter, if immediately thereafter the amount of its outstanding underwriting commitments, plus the value of its investments in securities of issuers (other than investment companies) of which it owns more than 10 per centum of the outstanding voting securities, exceeds
perturbation.justification: The original text sets a clear, objective limit on expenses (1.25%), while the changed text replaces it with a subjective standard determined by the Trust's board. This contradicts the intention of Section 12(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, as explained in the LLM's law explanation, which aims to prevent excessive charges through controls. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly state the need for objective limits, they reference Section 12(b) (15 USC 80a-12) and the Commission's authority to prescribe rules for investor protection. Allowing the board to subjectively determine expense limits could lead to excessive charges, undermining the investor protection that the Act intends to provide.
